Wind Financial Morning Post: June 17, 2026

Market Brief

National Statistics Bureau responds to multiple hot-button issues as May economic data is released
National Development and Reform Commission holds private enterprise symposium on the "Six Networks" initiative

China's housing market continues to recover, with 16 cities recording month-over-month price gains in new homes

US President Trump says Iran-US agreement negotiations will enter second phase, with Hormuz Strait to fully reopen before June 19

Fed's new chair Walsh prepares for first appearance, as markets await interest rate guidance

Bank of Japan raises rates by 25 basis points, returning to 1% for the first time in 31 years

Global equities diverge as Dow Jones hits new high, while crude oil prices plunge

Apple unveils 2027 product roadmap: camera-equipped earbuds and 20th anniversary iPhone set for launch
SpaceX shares rise for third straight day, market cap surpasses Amazon to enter top five global firms
DeepSeek secures its first funding round, with founder Liang Wenfeng personally investing approximately 20 billion yuan

Top Stories

China releases May economic data. In May, national industrial output above designated size rose 4.5% year-over-year, and the service production index increased 4.4%, both accelerating from April levels, with high-tech manufacturing output surging 15.1%. Social consumer goods retail sales fell 0.6% year-over-year. From January to May, fixed asset investment declined 4.1%, and real estate development investment dropped 16.2%; new home sales revenue fell 13.5%, with residential sales down 14.1%.

The State Council Information Office held a press conference on June 16, during which the National Statistics Bureau addressed key issues including growth momentum and new consumption metrics. The bureau stated that China's economy remained broadly stable in May, with clear signs of structural improvement. For the first time, the indicator for total social consumption of goods and services was published, offering a more comprehensive view of consumption. Data showed a 2.8% year-over-year increase in this metric from January to May. Regarding the decline in commodity retail sales, the bureau attributed it to a high base effect from last year and disruptions from heatwaves and heavy rainfall in some regions.

May housing price data for 70 major cities shows signs of stabilization. The National Statistics Bureau reported that 16 cities recorded month-over-month price increases in new residential properties, up two from April. In first-tier cities, new home prices rose 0.2% month-over-month for the fourth consecutive month; secondhand home prices climbed 0.4% month-over-month for the third consecutive month. Year-over-year price declines in first-tier cities narrowed further. However, second- and third-tier cities continued to see month-over-month price declines.

At 1:00 a.m. Beijing time on June 18, Fed's new chair Walsh will preside over his first Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) meeting and hold his first press conference. Markets expect the Fed to hold rates steady, with focus on inflation language, interest rate path guidance, whether to eliminate the dot plot, potential restart of balance sheet reduction, and how Powell will vote.

US President Trump said the Hormuz Strait will be fully reopened before June 19, and negotiations between the US and Iran will rapidly advance to the second phase. Trump stated Russia should reach an agreement with Ukraine and that US sanctions on Russian oil may soon be restored. Iran's Deputy Foreign Minister Lavanchi said the US has partially lifted its blockade. US media reported that Iran will be able to export oil immediately after signing the US-Iran agreement.

Iran's Foreign Minister Alaghehzi revealed that Iran and U.S. delegations will meet in Switzerland on June 19. The two sides' leaders will first sign a memorandum of understanding, followed by the first round of follow-up negotiations. The specific location in Switzerland will be announced separately.

China Macro & Policy

The 23rd meeting of the Standing Committee of the 14th National People's Congress will be held in Beijing from June 23 to 26. The meeting will review draft amendments to the Bidding and Tendering Law, the Financial Law, and the People's Bank of China Law submitted by the State Council.

National Development and Reform Commission (NDRC) Director Zheng Gajie chaired a private enterprise symposium on the coordinated development of the "Six Networks." Zheng stated that the NDRC will strengthen planning and construction of the "Six Networks," enhance inter-network coordination, and deploy government funds and new policy-based financial tools to generate more tangible output.

China's Foreign Minister Wang Yi spoke by phone with Pakistan's Deputy Prime Minister and Foreign Minister Dar. Wang congratulated Pakistan on facilitating the first-stage memorandum between Iran and the US, noting that the second-stage negotiations will be more challenging. China stands ready to work with Pakistan to continue promoting peace talks and contribute to lasting stability and development in the Middle East.

Data from the State Administration of Taxation shows that from January to May, revenue from industrial enterprises nationwide rose 6.8% year-over-year. Sales revenue in integrated circuits, optoelectronic devices, and semiconductor device manufacturing increased 57.7%, 32.6%, and 24.4% respectively. Sales revenue in robotics and intelligent vehicle equipment manufacturing rose 27.7% and 46.3% respectively.

China Equities Markets

A-share markets showed divergence: the SSE Index edged down 0.11% to 4,091.89, while the Shenzhen Component Index rose 0.93% and the ChiNext Index gained 1.72%. The Wind All China Index climbed 0.67% on trading volume of 3.09 trillion yuan. PCB-related stocks surged with multiple price limits; lithium battery, fiber optic, supercapacitor, optical communication, and storage chip stocks strengthened. Port and shipping sectors opened lower and continued to decline; coal stocks remained under pressure.

Hong Kong markets retreated sharply: the Hang Seng Index fell 1.4% to 24,493.95; the Hang Seng Tech Index dropped 2.24%; the Hang Seng China Enterprises Index slid 1.62%. Resource, tech, and property stocks led losses, while mainland brokerages outperformed. Net inflows via the Southbound Channel reached nearly 3.5 billion Hong Kong dollars. Jian Tao Jicengban and Jian Tao Group were heavily bought, while Tencent and Alibaba saw over 1.6 billion Hong Kong dollars in net selling.

As of June 16, Southbound funds have net inflowed over 3 billion Hong Kong dollars year-to-date. In the past month, information technology, industrials, and financials attracted the most Southbound inflows. Analysts expect Southbound funds to remain the largest source of incremental capital in Hong Kong this year, with projected net inflows of 310 to 440 billion Hong Kong dollars. Short-term market recovery is anticipated, with significant upside potential in the Hang Seng Tech Index.

Reports indicate that Xiaohongshu has hired Goldman Sachs and China International Capital Corporation to prepare for a potential Hong Kong IPO as early as this year. Sources say Xiaohongshu's private secondary market valuation reached $50 billion by the end of last year.

China Industry News

The Ministry of Human Resources and Social Security, the Ministry of Natural Resources, the National Development and Reform Commission, and the Ministry of Transport jointly issued a notice to support marine economic development and promote employment and entrepreneurship. The plan includes cultivating modern fisheries and expanding marine pharmaceuticals and biotechnology industries.

The State Administration for Market Regulation held a press conference announcing comprehensive measures to address internet advertising governance, "ghost restaurants," "618" e-commerce promotions, "phantom scales" in markets, and severe illegal失信list management.

In response to issues uncovered during a June 15 crackdown on "ghost restaurants," the Shenzhen Market Supervision Administration summoned executives from Meituan, Taobao Flash Purchase, and JD Waimai, demanding they assume primary responsibility by strictly prohibiting unlicensed, forged, or borrowed licenses and cross-store subcontracting.

The Ministry of Transport and seven other departments jointly released a multi-modal transport improvement plan, targeting major ports, key inland logistics hubs, large logistics parks, industrial clusters, and China-Europe (Asia) freight train hubs to accelerate upgrades and construction of key freight nodes.

IDC data shows global server market revenue reached $122.6 billion in Q1 2026, up 30.4% year-over-year. Dell accounted for 16.5% of the market, with a staggering 244.1% year-over-year growth, securing its position as the largest server OEM.

Fintech

The Digital Currency International Operations Center signed direct participant service agreements with 26 financial institutions. The platform's services will cover Hong Kong, Macau, Singapore, Laos, Thailand, the UAE, Qatar, and Brazil.

An AI version of Alipay, named "Abao," has been launched, allowing users to access over 10,000 services with a single command. The AI only serves as a navigation tool; all financial transactions require explicit user confirmation. The new version is currently in invitation-only testing and will be rolled out gradually.

WeChat Pay is testing an AI payment feature with Tencent's WorkBuddy, planning to launch an "AI-specific card" in WeChat Wallet as early as this week. The card's spending limit is determined by user-funded deposits, and AI-initiated payments require password verification.

Corporate News & Updates

Well-known Apple analyst Mark Gurman said Apple plans to launch AirPods with built-in cameras in the second half of 2027. Apple aims to release these new AirPods alongside its next-generation foldable iPhone and a special 20th anniversary iPhone model. All three products are reportedly in advanced development stages.

SpaceX's first acquisition since going public has been announced: it will acquire Anysphere, the developer of AI coding assistant Cursor, for $60 billion. The transaction is expected to close in Q3 2026 and will be executed entirely through stock issuance, with Anysphere shareholders receiving SpaceX Class A common shares.

DeepSeek has largely finalized its first funding round, with investors including founder Liang Wenfeng, Guozhi Investment, Tencent, NetEase, https://t.co/zDq3KwGBbd, and IDG Capital. The total fundraising amount is approximately 51 billion yuan, with a post-money valuation nearing 400 billion yuan. Liang personally contributed about 20 billion yuan, while Tencent invested approximately 10 billion yuan.

Industry insiders say Samsung Electronics will hold a global strategy meeting this week to discuss key business plans for the second half of the year. Its chip division meeting is scheduled for Thursday. Reports indicate Samsung has secured its first chip manufacturing order from Neuralink, Elon Musk's brain-computer interface firm.

Reports suggest Apple's first foldable iPhone Ultra has been delayed from its original fall 2026 launch to early 2027 due to hinge design and PCB process challenges. However, Apple supply chain sources responded: "False."

Yum! Brands announced it will sell its Pizza Hut business for $2.7 billion. Yum China will pay $1.2 billion in cash to acquire full ownership of Pizza Hut in mainland China, transitioning from exclusive franchisee to brand owner. Private equity firm LongRange will acquire Pizza Hut operations outside mainland China.

Global Macro & Policy

Israeli Air Force Commander Omri Tishler confirmed that Israel had planned a large-scale airstrike on hundreds of targets in Iran's interior on June 8, but the operation was canceled one hour before launch after US President Trump urged Israeli Prime Minister Netanyahu not to escalate conflict with Iran.

The European Parliament voted to approve legislation related to the EU-US trade agreement. The text will now be submitted to the European Council for final approval. US President Trump previously threatened to impose steep tariffs on EU exports if the agreement is not implemented by July 4.

European Central Bank Chief Economist Lane warned that inflationary pressures from Middle East conflicts have not yet fully permeated the economy, and the ECB must prepare for sustained inflation above target levels. Markets and economists widely expect at least one more ECB rate hike this year.

The Bank of Japan raised its policy rate by 25 basis points to 1%, the highest level in 31 years. The central bank also decided to suspend further tapering of its bond-buying program starting April 2027, maintaining monthly government bond purchases at around 2 trillion yen. Deputy Governor Shinichi Uchida emphasized the need to remain vigilant on inflation risks and signaled that further hikes remain possible depending on economic and price conditions.

The Reserve Bank of Australia held its benchmark rate steady at 4.35%, marking its first pause after three consecutive hikes. The RBA stressed that inflation remains too high and reiterated its readiness to raise rates again if needed to fulfill its mandate of price stability and full employment.

US import price index rose 1.9% month-over-month in May, matching April's revised figure. However, import prices rose 6.7% year-over-year, the largest gain in nearly four years. Increases in plastic raw materials, computer equipment, and international air transport costs were the primary drivers.

US new home starts plunged 15.4% month-over-month in May, annualizing at 1.18 million units—the lowest level since May 2020. Building permits also declined slightly by 0.7%, annualizing at 1.41 million units.

Global Equity Markets

US major indices closed mixed: the Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 0.64% to 51,999.67, setting a new record high; the S&P 500 fell 0.57% to 7,511.35; the Nasdaq Composite dropped 1.15% to 26,376.34. JPMorgan Chase rose over 3%, Visa climbed nearly 3%, leading the Dow. The Wind US Magnificent Seven Index declined 0.37%, with Nvidia down over 2% and Tesla off more than 1%. SpaceX gained 4.77%, closing at a $2.65 trillion market cap, surpassing Amazon to become the world's fifth-largest publicly traded company. The Nasdaq China Golden Dragon Index fell 2.5%, with Hesaitech down over 8% and Boss Zhipin off more than 7%.

European major indices closed slightly higher: Germany's DAX rose 0.07% to 24,910.41; France's CAC 40 gained 0.75% to 8,447.27; the UK's FTSE 100 advanced 0.61% to 10,494.21. The temporary Iran-US agreement to reopen the Hormuz Strait lifted risk sentiment, while falling oil prices eased inflation concerns.

Asia-Pacific indices closed higher: Japan's Nikkei 225 rose 0.13% to 69,404.50, setting a new closing record and briefly surpassing 70,000 intraday. South Korea's KOSPI climbed 2.11% to 8,726.60. India's S&P BSE SENSEX gained 0.71% to 76,808.48.

Reports suggest SK Hynsis plans to unveil a shareholder return program of up to 100 trillion won ($66.4 billion) after its US listing, encompassing share buybacks and dividends. SK Hynsis stated it has not discussed the specific scale of the proposed return.

Goldman Sachs' global M&A advisory volume has surpassed $1 trillion this year, reaching that milestone nearly a month ahead of its 2021 peak, with the runner-up trailing by over $300 billion. Regulatory easing, abundant financing, and AI-driven industry transformation have significantly boosted corporate M&A appetite. Goldman Sachs remains optimistic about M&A prospects.

Bonds

China's interbank bond market saw heightened activity, with major interest rate yields generally declining. Five- and seven-year government bond yields, and five- and ten-year policy bank bond yields all fell by more than 2 basis points. Treasury futures closed higher, with the 30-year contract up 0.22%. The People's Bank of China conducted 449.5 billion yuan in reverse repurchase operations, injecting 296.5 billion yuan net liquidity, but funding conditions remained tight as tax payments approached.

The Ministry of Finance successfully issued 15 billion yuan in offshore RMB sovereign bonds in Hong Kong, with a subscription multiple of 3.86x. The issuance included 4.5 billion yuan of two-year bonds at 1.29%, 4.5 billion yuan of three-year bonds at 1.33%, 3.5 billion yuan of five-year bonds at 1.49%, and 2.5 billion yuan of ten-year bonds at 1.80%.

Approved by the State Council, the Ministry of Finance will issue euro-denominated sovereign bonds in Luxembourg next week, with a size not exceeding 5 billion euros.

China Central Depository & Clearing Co. plans to further reduce settlement fees for market makers, lowering the discount on bond trading settlement fees from 80% to 75% for transactions executed via market making. The incentive will be effective from July 1, 2026, to December 31, 2028.

US Treasury yields declined across the curve: the 2-year yield fell 0.84 basis points to 4.054%; the 3-year yield dropped 1.41 basis points to 4.094%; the 5-year yield fell 2.82 basis points to 4.162%; the 10-year yield declined 3.36 basis points to 4.439%; the 30-year yield fell 3.81 basis points to 4.942%.

Commodities

Precious metals futures closed mixed: COMEX gold rose 0.03% to $4,353.00 per ounce; COMEX silver fell 0.08% to $70.12 per ounce. Persistent global central bank buying expectations and eased geopolitical tensions provided support, offsetting uncertainty from divergent Fed policy signals.

US crude oil futures fell 5.11% to $76.62 per barrel; Brent crude dropped 4.61% to $79.34 per barrel. The US-Iran memorandum of understanding and confirmation of the Hormuz Strait's reopening signaled immediate Iranian oil exports, causing a sharp retreat in risk premiums. Market expectations of a surge in supply led to profit-taking, with major banks lowering oil price forecasts and driving prices lower.

Most base metals on the LME rose: LME lead gained 0.63% to $1,982.50/ton; LME copper rose 0.52% to $13,816.50/ton; LME aluminum climbed 0.34% to $3,391.00/ton; LME nickel advanced 0.25% to $17,955.00/ton; LME tin fell 0.49% to $55,030.00/ton; LME zinc declined 0.79% to $3,561.50/ton.

The World Gold Council's 2026 Global Central Bank Gold Reserves Survey showed that 89% of reserve managers expect global central bank gold holdings to increase over the next 12 months. 84% of respondents believe gold's share in global reserves will rise over the next five years, up from 76% last year.

FX & Currency Markets

On Tuesday, the onshore CNY/USD rate closed at 6.7574, down 2 basis points from the previous close; the offshore rate closed at 6.7575. The PBOC's central parity rate was set at 6.8108, a 20-basis-point depreciation from the prior day.

At New York close, the US Dollar Index fell 0.11% to 99.56. Most non-US currencies rose: EUR/USD gained 0.16% to 1.1608; GBP/USD rose 0.11% to 1.3427; AUD/USD fell 0.05% to 0.7067; USD/JPY edged up 0.07% to 160.4650; USD/CAD rose 0.03% to 1.3995; USD/CHF fell 0.19% to 0.7932; offshore CNY/USD rose 26 basis points to 6.7570.

Sludge was founded by David Moore and Donny Shaw, who formerly ran OpenCongressdotorg, a website for tracking money in the U.S. Congress. OpenCongress, which has since been shut down, was a joint project of the Sunlight Foundation and the Participatory Politics Foundation (PPF), both left-of-center advocacy organizations. Moore continues to serve as Executive Director of PPF, which has the stated goal of “advocating for full public financing of elections.”

Shaw is a journalist who has covered corporate involvement in politics for such outlets as the Daily Beast and HuffPost. At HuffPost, he co-wrote a piece pressuring the President of Brown University to step down from a position on the board of Goldman Sachs because of the company’s business practices. 

In January 2018, Sludge was announced as one of 13 online publications in the “first fleet” of outlets on the new journalism platform Civil. The Civil platform is owned by the Civil Media Company, which is funded primarily by Canadian billionaire and cryptocurrency entrepreneur Joseph Lubin. Lubin’s company ConsenSys provided $2.5 million in cash to help launch Civil in October 2017, with an additional $2.5 million in in-kind services such as office space.

Civil Media Company gave each Civil newsroom including Sludge an initial grant by the consisting of a mix of cash and cryptocurrency “tokens,” symbolizing an ownership stake in Civil. Moore told media outlet Ars Technica that the cash portion of Sludge’s initial grant consisted of “enough for five full-time reporters to be paid in cash salaries.” These funds ran out in 2019, according to Moore’s note on Kickstarter that solicited crowdfunding donations to keep the publication operational.

Sludge’s website consists of five sections, representing the various focuses of its reporting: “Climate,” its section detailing political and advocacy spending of the oil and natural gas industry; “Defense,” focusing on the defense industry; “Hate,” which attacks right-of-center advocacy using Southern Poverty Law Center-style guilt-by-association tactics; “Influence,” a catchall category for lobbying; and “Tech,” detailing spending by technology companies.

This is the seventh report of many reports in this series that I will be publishing.

6.16.26

GEOPOLITICS & ENERGY SHOCK
- US-Iran Conflict & Breakthrough: Markets are digesting a massive breakthrough after President Trump announced late Sunday on Truth Social that a "complete" interim deal with Iran has been finalized. The agreement halts hostilities immediately on all fronts (including Lebanon) and outlines a 60-day negotiation window focused on Tehran's nuclear program. A formal signing ceremony is expected this Friday in Switzerland, hosted by Pakistan.
- Strait of Hormuz Reopening: Trump announced he has authorized the toll-free reopening of the Strait of Hormuz, with the US naval blockade set to end. However, shipowners, insurers, and maritime analysts remain highly cautious. Normalizing traffic and clearing mines from the waterway among the ~600 stranded vessels is expected to take months.
- Nuclear & Sanctions Friction: The deal defers the fate of Iran's nuclear program to technical talks. Trump told the NY Times he will instantly restart military strikes if Iran fails to reach a final nuclear accord. Iranian media claims $24B in frozen assets will be released over the 60 days (50% upfront) alongside oil sanctions waivers, though US officials insist relief is strictly performance-dependent. Regional risk remains high as Israeli PM Netanyahu told Trump that Israel is not bound by the Lebanon clause and will continue its southern campaign against Hezbollah.
- Crude Oil Collapse: WTI and Brent crude plummeted nearly 5% to three-month lows, with Brent dropping below $82/bbl and testing its 200-DMA for the first time since February. Middle Eastern physical crude benchmarks collapsed, and the forward curve for Dubai and Murban flipped into contango (prompt discount), signaling a major easing of immediate supply anxiety. Both Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley aggressively cut their structural oil price outlooks.
- Regional Energy Plays: Qatar plans a rapid expansion of LNG exports once safe passage is normalized, targeting 50% capacity within one month and 80% within two months. Concurrently, Canadian wildfires in Alberta are burning within 11-19 km of critical oil sands facilities, threatening 500,000 bpd of crude production. 
- WTI OpEx: The front-month futures roll occurs today at 2:30 p.m. ET as August crude (CLQ6) becomes the front-month contract over July (CLN6).

MACROECONOMICS, RATES & FEDERAL RESERVE
- The Warsh FOMC Debut: Newly sworn-in Fed Chair Kevin Warsh faces his maiden policy meeting on Wednesday. While the central bank is universally expected to hold rates flat at 3.50%-3.75% (>98% probability), the updated Summary of Economic Projections (SEP) and dot plot are expected to show a hawkish revision. 
- Rate Hike Adjustments: Following May's blockbuster payroll print (unemployment steady at 4.3%), macro models have aggressively shifted. Swap markets are currently pricing a 5% chance of a hike this week, a 50% chance by October, and an 80% chance by year-end. Policymakers enter a media blackout as investors look to see if Warsh will roll back forward guidance and dismantle the traditional dot plot architecture.
- Inflation Shock Day: US May CPI prints today at 8:30 a.m. ET, with the annual headline figure projected to jump to a 3-year high of 4.2% on war-driven energy, jet fuel, and metal spikes. JPMorgan's Market Intelligence desk warns that a core MoM print above 0.35% could trigger an immediate 3% drop in the S&P 500, while a standard print implies a 1% index move.
- Volatility Curve Inversion: The CBOE Volatility Index (VIX) plunged 5.2% to 16.76 in a sharp reversal from recent highs above 23.38. However, near-term anxiety remains intense, with the 3-month VIX curve shifting into backwardation. 
- International Central Banking: 
  - BOJ: Normalizing policy in a 7-1 vote, the Bank of Japan raised its benchmark interest rate to a 31-year high of 1.0% to combat structural energy inflation, though it paused its bond taper program to continue buying 2 trillion yen in JGBs monthly. Dovish board member Toichiro Asada dissented.
  - ECB: President Lagarde defended recent rate hikes to 2.25%, warning that indirect energy shocks are embedding across European travel services and utilities. The Bundesbank slashed German growth forecasts, warning of prolonged stagnation.
  - Bank of Canada: Expected to hold its key policy rate steady at 2.25% on Wednesday. The Canadian economy has entered a technical recession (two straight quarters of contraction), though institutions like CIBC expect the bank to downplay the slide due to a strong Q2 rebound.

EQUITIES, SPACE MANIA & THE AI CAPITAL RECALIBRATION
- SpaceX Multi-Trillion Run: Following its blockbuster public debut, SpaceX rocketed 20% in a single session, adding $433 billion in market value (the second-largest one-day market cap gain in US corporate history). SpaceX sits at a $2.538 trillion valuation—just 4% behind Amazon. Elon Musk announced that SpaceX could hit $1 trillion in annual revenue by 2030, hitting a post-IPO valuation over $2 trillion, despite currently posting a $4.94 billion net loss on $18.67 billion in 2025 revenue.
- Anysphere Acquisition: Broadening its enterprise AI stack, SpaceX acquired Anysphere, the startup behind the popular AI coding assistant Cursor, for $60 billion. Retail demand for SpaceX stock has been historic, matching the entire retail purchasing volume of the US stock market last week. Listed options launch today, featuring call strikes stretching to $380 for this Thursday's expiration.
- Frontier AI Listings: The era of stock scarcity has broken. OpenAI has confidentially filed for its US IPO, while rival Anthropic prepares a concurrent listing, both eyeing ~$1 trillion public valuations. Databricks is in discussions for a fresh funding round at a $ 165B-$ 175B valuation, targeting an IPO next year. China's top AI startup, DeepSeek, finalized a massive $7.4 billion funding round backed by Tencent and CATL, valuing the entity at over $50 billion.
- The Token Minimization Era: In his closing analysis, Mike O'Rorke highlights a fundamental structural shift in corporate artificial intelligence adoption. Major hyperscalers (Microsoft, Meta, Amazon) are executing "Tokenminimizing" strategies, actively rationing expensive AI consumption and switching to less expensive, "good enough" open models. This emergence of an LLM pricing war—coupled with Oracle guiding its fiscal capex up 27%—signals that enterprise infrastructure monetization will take much longer to materialize than ebullient consensus models assume.
- Anthropic National Security Ban: Adding a major bottleneck to the tech sector, the US government issued an emergency export control order forcing Anthropic to completely block all foreign nationals from accessing its advanced "Mythos" and "Fable" AI models. The restriction was triggered by Amazon researchers proving that safety guardrails could be bypassed to uncover sovereign software vulnerabilities. 
- Corporate Equity Supply: Goldman Sachs warns that a historic deluge of ~$1.5 trillion in new equity issuance via mega-IPOs and corporate secondary sales over the next two years will fundamentally reverse the 20-year tailwind of net-negative US stock supply. 
- Technical Index Flows: The CRSP Index Reconstitution begins today through June 22, directly impacting over $1 trillion in active and passive US domestic equities. Marvell (MRVL) and Flex (FLEX) are surging premarket on news they will join the S&P 500 later this month.

MARKET MOVERS & CORPORATE ACTIONS
- Energy Retrenchment vs. Travel Surge: Battered travel, airline, and hospitality names are leading premarket gainers on the back of the peace deal (United +4.3%, Delta +3.9%, American +3.5%, Norwegian Cruise +4.0%). Conversely, megacap oil and exploration names are sliding heavily (Occidental -3.3%, Exxon -2.5%, Chevron -2.4%). 
- Mining Inflows: Easing Middle East tensions have allowed spot gold to stabilize around $4,330/bbl, allowing precious metal miners to catch a strong bid (Pan American Silver +4.8%, Newmont +4.7%, Barrick +4.3%).
- M&A & Antitrust Clearances: 
  - FOXA/ROKU: Fox Corp officially agreed to acquire Roku in a cash-and-stock transaction valued at $160/share ($22 billion enterprise value). The deal will be backed by $12 billion in committed bridge financing.
  - PARA/WBD: The US Department of Justice completed its 8-month antitrust review and fully cleared Paramount's massive $110 billion acquisition of Warner Bros. Discovery.
  - Woodside Energy (WDS): Denied it is holding active talks with ExxonMobil, though internal leaks show Exxon is evaluating the Australian LNG giant as an acquisition target.
- Super Micro Offering: Super Micro Computer (SMCI) announced a giant $7 billion capital raise—comprising a $5 billion underwritten package and a $2 billion open-market ATM program—to secure liquid-cooling components and hardware for its enterprise AI server backlogs.
- GitLab (GTLB): Beating Q1 expectations with revenue of $264.2 million, the software platform raised its full-year guidance on agentic AI tailwinds but simultaneously announced a 14% workforce reduction and exit from 22 countries.

DOMESTIC & GLOBAL POLITICS
- Multi-State Primaries Today: High-stakes primary voting commences today in Maine, South Carolina, Nevada, and North Dakota. In Maine, five-term Republican Senator Susan Collins faces intense reelection pressure over macro inflation, while her challenger Graham Platner battles a public texting scandal. In South Carolina, Rep. Nancy Mace faces a critical base test after demanding the release of Jeffrey Epstein documents.
- UK Social Media Ban: Prime Minister Keir Starmer announced an aggressive "full ban" prohibiting children under the age of 16 from using social media platforms, targeting stranger interaction and livestreaming mechanisms by spring 2027.
- USMCA Integration: Ontario Premier Doug Ford is heading to Washington to launch a "Fortress North America" integration pitch with US officials ahead of the July 1 USMCA revision deadline.
- Swiss Population Referendum: Switzerland holds a national referendum this Sunday on a hard population cap of 10 million people, a right-wing push that corporations warn could trigger severe labor and macroeconomic restrictions.

CALENDAR

- Tue (16th): China Retail Sales, Industrial Production, Fixed-Asset Investment, Property Prices. Germany ZEW Survey. US Housing Starts & Building Permits, Import Price Index. 🔔 Closing Bell Recap: Tech Takes A Breather While Falling Oil Fuels Rotation
Tuesday, June 16, 2026

Hello traders and investors,

After Monday's powerful advance, the market spent Tuesday shifting gears.

Technology stocks, particularly semiconductors, finally ran into some profit-taking after a strong rebound over the past several sessions. That weakness weighed on the S&P 500 and Nasdaq throughout the day, but beneath the surface the story was much different.

Falling oil prices continued to improve sentiment across several economically sensitive sectors, Treasury yields moved lower again, and investors rotated into financials, industrials, and materials.

The result was a session that felt more like a pause in leadership rather than a breakdown in market confidence.

📊 Mixed Session For Major Indexes

The major averages finished split as investors rotated away from technology and toward other areas of the market.

🟢 Dow Jones Industrial Average: +0.6%

🔴 S&P 500: -0.6%

🔴 Nasdaq Composite: -1.2%

The Dow continued its march deeper into record territory, while the technology-heavy Nasdaq absorbed the bulk of the pressure from semiconductor weakness.

Small and mid-cap stocks also finished lower.

🔴 Russell 2000: -0.9%

🔴 S&P MidCap 400: -0.3%

💻 Semiconductors Give Back Monday's Gains

The biggest source of weakness came from the technology sector.

🔻 Information Technology: -2.3%

After gaining 3.4% on Monday, the sector experienced a sharp reversal as traders locked in profits across semiconductor names.

The PHLX Semiconductor Index fell 5.7%, surrendering all of Monday's advance.

Several chip-related names finished among the market's worst performers.

🔻 Monolithic Power Systems (MPWR): -9.4%

🔻 Lumentum (LITE): -8.6%

The selling was broad enough to pressure the entire technology complex and weighed heavily on the major averages throughout the day.

🤖 NVIDIA And Mega-Caps Lose Momentum

NVIDIA was among the notable laggards inside the Magnificent Seven group.

🔻 NVIDIA (NVDA): -2.4%

The weakness contributed to a mostly lower session for mega-cap growth stocks and pushed the Vanguard Mega Cap Growth ETF down 1.1%.

After carrying much of the market's leadership over recent weeks, Tuesday's session reflected investors taking some profits and reallocating capital elsewhere.

🚀 SpaceX Continues To Defy Gravity

One stock that refused to participate in the technology retreat was SpaceX.

Despite giving back much of an early advance during the afternoon, buyers returned aggressively into the closing bell.

🚀 SpaceX (SPCX): +5.0%

The stock has now extended its remarkable post-IPO run and continues attracting significant investor attention as one of the market's newest mega-cap names.

Its ability to attract fresh buyers while much of technology moved lower stood out throughout the session.

🛢️ Oil Prices Fall Below $77

The market's most important tailwind remained intact.

Crude oil dropped another $4.84 per barrel.

🔻 Crude Oil: $76.06

🔻 Daily Change: -6.0%

Investors continue betting that Friday's scheduled signing of the U.S.-Iran peace agreement will reduce geopolitical risk and keep global energy supplies flowing smoothly.

The decline in oil prices has become one of the market's most powerful supportive forces, helping ease inflation concerns while improving the outlook for several cyclical industries.

🏦 Financials Lead The Market

With technology under pressure, investors looked elsewhere.

The Financials sector emerged as the day's strongest performer.

🟢 Financials: +1.5%

JPMorgan Chase led the Dow after Bloomberg reported that L3Harris selected JPMorgan and Morgan Stanley to lead a potential $2 billion IPO of its missile business, Axyv.

🟢 JPMorgan Chase (JPM): +3.7%

🟢 Morgan Stanley (MS): +1.3%

🟢 L3Harris Technologies (LHX): +2.1%

The news helped drive strength throughout the banking sector.

✈️ Industrials Continue Attracting Buyers

Industrials remained another area of strength.

🟢 Industrials: +0.7%

Most companies within the sector finished higher, benefiting from lower oil prices and continued improvement in economic sentiment.

Several electrical product manufacturers also found support from their close relationship to semiconductor-related demand trends.

🏗️ Materials Extend Their Strong Run

Materials posted another positive session.

🟢 Materials: +0.5%

Construction-material companies continued attracting buyers as Treasury yields moved lower.

The sector has quietly become one of the stronger performers during the market's recent broadening-out phase.

📉 Energy Falls With Oil

Not surprisingly, the Energy sector moved in the opposite direction.

🔻 Energy: -0.4%

It was the only sector besides technology to finish with a loss greater than 0.1%.

The continued collapse in crude prices weighed heavily on energy producers even as it supported most other areas of the market.

🧬 Moderna And CoreWeave Stand Out

Corporate news remained relatively light, but a few names generated significant interest.

🚀 Moderna (MRNA): +6.3%

Shares rallied following positive pipeline and commercialization updates.

🚀 CoreWeave (CRWV): +9.7%

The company announced it delivered the fastest DeepSeek-V3 671B training performance in the benchmark, helping shares post one of the largest gains of the day.

🏠 Housing Data Disappoints

Tuesday's economic calendar included several housing-related reports.

Housing Starts

Actual: 1.177 million

Consensus: 1.440 million

Previous revised: 1.392 million

Housing starts came in significantly below expectations.

A major source of weakness came from multi-unit construction, which declined 40.2% month over month.

Single-family starts in the South, the country's largest homebuilding region, also fell 5.2%.

Building Permits

Actual: 1.413 million

Consensus: 1.410 million

Previous revised: 1.423 million

Building permits were essentially in line with expectations.

🌎 Trade Prices Remain Elevated

Additional inflation-related data showed continued pricing pressure.

Import Prices

Actual: +1.9%

Prior revised: +2.0%

Import Prices Ex-Oil

Actual: +0.8%

Export Prices

Actual: +1.3%

Export Prices Ex-Agriculture

Actual: +1.2%

The data suggests pricing pressures remain present throughout global trade channels even as energy prices decline.

📉 Treasury Yields Continue Moving Lower

Treasuries extended their recent advance.

The 2-Year Treasury Yield fell two basis points to 4.05%.

The 10-Year Treasury Yield declined four basis points to 4.43%.

The 30-year Treasury yield finished at its lowest closing level since late April.

Strong demand for a $22 billion 20-year Treasury auction also supported the move.

☕ My Take

Today's session felt much healthier than the headline index performance might suggest.

Yes, the Nasdaq was lower.

Yes, semiconductors experienced a meaningful pullback.

But when one sector gives back gains and money simply rotates into other areas rather than leaving the market altogether, that's a very different situation than broad-based selling.

Financials were strong.

Industrials were strong.

Materials were strong.

Oil continued falling.

Treasury yields continued falling.

Those aren't characteristics of a market under major stress.

The biggest takeaway from Tuesday is that investors appear willing to keep putting money to work, they're simply becoming more selective after the powerful rally we saw Monday.

Tomorrow's FOMC meeting now becomes the next major focal point.

text:
过去几周，看了Bloomberg连续几份报告

市场表面上在交易战争、油价和美联储。

实际上，全球资本正在以前所未有的速度押注AI基础设施。

而这正在创造一个越来越极端的市场。

先看几个数据。

美国CPI重新超过4%。

30年期美债收益率一度突破5.19%，创2007年以来新高。

全球基金经理调查显示，4月股票加仓幅度创历史纪录。

与此同时，越来越多投资者认为未来一年最可能出现的宏观环境是：

增长放缓 + 通胀顽固。

也就是滞胀。

按理说，这种组合不应该对应历史新高的股市。

但市场依然不断上涨。

为什么？

答案只有两个字：

AI。

Bloomberg统计发现，MSCI ACWI全球2513家上市公司中，今年盈利预测上调的5800亿美元里，75%来自排名前1%的公司。

前1%公司的2027年盈利预测今年被上调33%。

剩余99%的公司仅上调3%。

换句话说：

全球股市越来越不像“全球经济上涨”。

而更像：

少数AI赢家在拉着整个指数前进。

这种集中度已经越来越接近2000年互联网泡沫时期。

更有意思的是，AI牛市内部也开始发生变化。

过去两年市场认为：

微软、谷歌、Meta、亚马逊这些平台公司将成为最大赢家。

但资金已经开始转向半导体。

如果今年做多SOX半导体指数，同时做空Magnificent 7组合，累计收益率达到71%。

如果做多SOX同时做空金融板块，收益率达到92.3%。

AI行情已经从软件阶段进入硬件阶段。

Micron单日上涨19.3%。

SK Hynix突破1万亿美元市值。

三星也已经重返万亿美元俱乐部。

Bloomberg统计显示，今年推动标普500上涨的最大力量已经不是互联网平台，而是芯片公司。

市场越来越相信：

AI时代真正赚钱的是卖铲子的人。

而不是淘金的人。

背后的原因也很简单。

AI资本开支正在进入历史级别扩张。

高盛预计：

2027年全球超大云厂商资本开支将达到1.1万亿美元。

高于许多发达国家GDP。

微软、谷歌、Meta、亚马逊等公司的资本开支占经营现金流比例已经接近100%。

2023年这个数字还只有40%。

换句话说：

这些公司赚到的现金，几乎全部重新投入数据中心、GPU和AI基础设施。

整个行业正在进行一场前所未有的军备竞赛。

而这场竞赛还有第二个维度：

中国。

Bloomberg近期特别强调：

美国领先大模型（Brains）。

中国领先Physical AI（Body）。

人形机器人成本结构中：

机械执行系统占50%。

AI模型占35%。

电池仅占15%。

中国在执行器、电机、机械系统、稀土和制造链方面拥有明显优势。

未来AI竞争未必只是ChatGPT和GPU。

更可能是：

机器人。

工业自动化。

现实世界部署。

美国负责大脑。

中国负责身体。

与此同时，地缘政治并没有变得更安全。

霍尔木兹海峡危机虽然暂时缓解。

布伦特原油重新跌破90美元。

但Bloomberg认为，真正的赢家是伊朗。

美国放弃政权更替目标。

被冻结资金开始释放。

霍尔木兹未来甚至可能成为收费通道。

更重要的是：

全球开始意识到一个现实。

控制关键卡口的国家，正在重新获得定价权。

中国拥有稀土。

伊朗拥有霍尔木兹。

全球化并未结束。

但成本正在上升。

而这意味着未来几年：

贸易成本更高。

能源成本更高。

资本成本更高。

最后回到市场。

如果把最近十几份Bloomberg报告浓缩成一句话：

全球投资者已经默认AI会改变世界。

真正的争议已经不再是技术。

而是估值。

今天的市场价格，是否已经提前反映了未来十年的成功？

这是当前所有资产背后最重要的问题。

#Macro #AI #Semiconductor #China #Investing #Markets #Nvidia #Micron #PhysicalAI

VVIX sits at 87.69. VVIX measures how nervous traders are about VIX itself spiking. The historical average is around 85, so 87.69 sits in normal calm range. ...pay less at the pump. But the bigger story is what this signals about the global economy.

When two of Wall Street's biggest banks revise their price predictions in the same direction at the same time, it means they've both concluded demand is weaker than they thought. That's not a technical adjustment. That's a view on where the world economy is heading.

The last time Brent spent a sustained period below $80 was 2023, when fears about slowing Chinese industrial demand were peaking. Those same concerns are back on the table now.

The hidden winner here is anyone who pays an energy bill or fills a tank. Lower crude prices feed through to petrol forecourts within days. The hidden loser is every oil-producing nation that built its budget around $85 or higher.

The rebound question is real though. If OPEC cuts supply in response, the price floor could reassert itself fast. For now, cheaper oil is the call from the people with the most skin in the game.
